name: MeToo
outcome_summary: Major cultural shift plus substantive policy wins on NDAs and workplace harassment law, but structural power in entertainment, media, and politics largely intact. Weinstein's NY conviction overturned in 2024. Time's Up shut down in 2023. The cultural conversation was permanently altered; the power structures were not.

progress_markers.event: Tarana Burke coins 'Me Too' on Myspace, focused on young Black and brown survivors in low-income communities
Dylan Farrow's open letter about Woody Allen; Cosby accusers speak publicly — momentum builds with no institutional response
Access Hollywood tape demonstrates elite impunity; Roger Ailes forced from Fox News after harassment settlements revealed
Ronan Farrow/NYT Weinstein exposé Oct 5-10; Alyssa Milano's tweet Oct 15 — 19 million uses of hashtag in 24 hours
Dozens of high-profile firings within weeks: Matt Lauer (NBC), Charlie Rose (CBS), Louis C.K., Kevin Spacey, Al Franken
Time's Up legal defense fund launched Jan 1; $24M raised for low-income survivors' legal costs
12+ US states pass NDA reform laws restricting secret settlements in harassment cases
Aziz Ansari and Junot Diaz cases generate 'witch hunt' and 'due process' counter-narrative; movement fragmentation
Harvey Weinstein convicted of rape and sexual assault in New York; sentenced to 23 years
Speak Out Act passes US Congress restricting NDAs in workplace sexual harassment cases
Ending Forced Arbitration of Sexual Assault and Sexual Harassment Act signed — survivors can access courts
Time's Up foundation shuts down after leadership scandals and financial mismanagement revelations
Weinstein convicted in Los Angeles on additional rape charges, sentenced to 16 years
New York Court of Appeals overturns Weinstein NY conviction on grounds of improper testimony; retrial ordered

lead_orgs.name: MeToo Movement (Tarana Burke)
lead_orgs.description: Original org, community-based, focused on Black and brown survivors; Burke reclaimed leadership after 2017

lead_orgs.name: Time's Up
lead_orgs.description: Hollywood-led legal defense fund raising $24M; created industry accountability mechanisms; shut down 2023

lead_orgs.name: RAINN
lead_orgs.description: Largest anti-sexual violence org in US; provided survivor support infrastructure during tipping moment

funders: individual Hollywood donors (Shonda Rhimes, Ava DuVernay)

villains: Fox News (Roger Ailes/Bill O'Reilly NDA culture)
allies: Multiple studios adopting mandatory harassment training

international: UN Women (issued statement and tracked legislation)
ILO Convention 190 (2019) on violence at work directly linked to MeToo momentum

what_actually_changed: The US legal landscape on workplace harassment has materially changed: NDAs can no longer silence sexual harassment victims in many states, forced arbitration clauses are unenforceable for sexual assault claims, and dozens of serial predators lost careers. However, structural power in entertainment and media institutions was not dismantled — Hollywood leadership remains predominantly male, and Time's Up collapsed by 2023. Weinstein's New York conviction being overturned represents the limits of criminal accountability even in the highest-profile case.

name: Black Lives Matter — 2020 Tipping
outcome_summary: The largest protest movement in American history generated enormous institutional pledges and some policy changes, but most concrete policing reforms were reversed by 2021-2022. BLMGNF financial scandals damaged the organization. Cultural shift is permanent and generational; structural change to policing is minimal. The 2024 election saw backlash politics partly fueled by anti-BLM sentiment.

progress_markers.event: BLM founded by Patrisse Cullors, Alicia Garza, Opal Tometi after Trayvon Martin acquittal
Michael Brown (Ferguson) and Eric Garner killings; Ferguson uprising; BLM chapter network begins building in 30+ cities
Alton Sterling and Philando Castile killings; Colin Kaepernick kneels during NFL anthem — broadens cultural front
COVID reveals racial health disparities; Breonna Taylor killed March 13; Ahmaud Arbery killed Feb 23
George Floyd killed May 25; bystander video of Derek Chauvin kneeling on neck for 8:46 min; protests in all 50 states within days
15-26 million US participants; 4,000+ protests in 60 countries; largest protest movement in US history
$10.6 billion pledged to racial justice orgs by major corporations and foundations
Minneapolis City Council voted to defund police; Portland, NYC, LA cut budgets; dozens of cities adopted reform measures
Confederate statues removed across US and UK (Cecil Rhodes, Edward Colston in Bristol)
Derek Chauvin convicted of murder April 20 — rare conviction of a police officer
Minneapolis voters reject ballot measure to replace police department Nov 2021; most 'defund' measures reversed
BLMGNF financial scandal: Patrisse Cullors accused of misusing $6M in funds; resignations and factional splits
SCOTUS ends affirmative action; DEI programs rolled back at major corporations under political pressure
Trump administration executive orders ban DEI in federal agencies; private sector follows; many police reform laws repealed

lead_orgs.name: Black Lives Matter Global Network Foundation (BLMGNF)
lead_orgs.description: Central org founded by Cullors, Garza, Tometi; raised $90M in 2020; faced major financial scandal 2021-22

lead_orgs.name: Movement for Black Lives (M4BL)
lead_orgs.description: Coalition of 150+ organizations with comprehensive policy platform; produced detailed defund/invest demands

lead_orgs.name: Color of Change
lead_orgs.description: Digital racial justice org; major fundraiser and policy advocate; 7M+ members

celebrities: virtually entire Hollywood and music industry
government_champions: Rep. Karen Bass (D-CA) — George Floyd Justice in Policing Act passed House 2021

villains: Police unions (NYPD PBA, LAPD union) — blocked reform

international: UN High Commissioner for Human Rights called for inquiry
Council of Europe resolutions on racial profiling

what_actually_changed: Derek Chauvin's conviction set a precedent for police accountability, and some jurisdictions passed meaningful reform laws (Minnesota, Colorado, California). The cultural conversation around systemic racism reached mainstream audiences at unprecedented scale, and 'police abolition' entered the policy debate. However, most 'defund' measures were reversed within 18 months, the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act never passed the Senate, BLMGNF's financial scandals damaged credibility, and the subsequent backlash contributed to the 2024 rightward political shift. The generational consciousness change is real; the structural change to American policing is minimal.

name: Plastic Straw / Single-Use Plastic Movement
outcome_summary: Major corporate and legislative action on single-use plastics globally — 70+ countries banned plastic straws and/or bags. EU Single-Use Plastics Directive 2021 is comprehensive legislation. But straws represent approximately 0.025% of ocean plastic by weight; the narrative win exceeded the material win. The global plastics treaty collapsed in 2024. Ocean plastic levels still rising.

progress_markers.event: Captain Charles Moore discovers Great Pacific Garbage Patch; ocean plastic enters scientific mainstream
California first US state to ban single-use plastic bags; demonstrates legislative viability
Marine biologist Christine Figgener's video removing plastic straw from sea turtle nostril goes viral — 28M+ views; emotional trigger crystallized
Break Free From Plastic coalition formed globally; 1,300+ organizations; NGO infrastructure ready to convert attention to policy
UK PM Theresa May announces intention to eliminate avoidable plastic waste; Scotland announces ban
Starbucks pledges to eliminate plastic straws by 2020; McDonald's follows; corporate pledge race begins
Seattle and San Francisco become first major US cities to ban plastic straws
EU Single-Use Plastics Directive adopted — bans 10 most common single-use plastic items
Disability advocates object that straw bans harm people who need flexible plastic straws; exemptions required, complicating enforcement
EU ban enters force; 70+ countries have some form of single-use plastic legislation
UNEA resolution to negotiate global plastics treaty — first potential legally binding international instrument
Starbucks paper straws fail; company abandons for strawless lids; consumer frustration with alternatives undermines narrative
Global Plastics Treaty negotiations collapse in Busan, South Korea — oil-producing nations block binding reduction targets
Treaty negotiations continue; ocean plastic levels still rising despite bans; upstream production not addressed

lead_orgs.name: Break Free From Plastic
lead_orgs.description: Global coalition of 1,300+ orgs; annual brand audit documenting top plastic polluters by volume

lead_orgs.name: Ocean Conservancy
lead_orgs.description: Led public awareness and coastal cleanup campaigns; data on beach plastic quantities

lead_orgs.name: Plastic Pollution Coalition
lead_orgs.description: US-based advocacy connecting consumer to corporate to policy campaigns

celebrities: David Attenborough (Blue Planet II 2017 — pivotal documentary)

villains: Coca-Cola, PepsiCo, Nestle (top plastic polluters per annual brand audits)

what_actually_changed: Plastic straw bans exist in 70+ countries and thousands of cities, and the EU Single-Use Plastics Directive is genuine binding legislation covering 10 product categories. Consumer consciousness about single-use plastic has fundamentally shifted, and 'single-use' is now a regulated legal category. However, straws are less than 0.025% of ocean plastic by weight, the global plastics treaty collapsed in 2024, and ocean plastic levels continue rising because the campaign was more effective at banning visible consumer items than addressing upstream industrial production. A massive narrative win with modest material impact.

name: Fridays for Future
outcome_summary: The largest climate mobilization in history and a generation of youth climate activists normalized as political actors. 2,000+ climate emergency declarations. The EU Green Deal and US Inflation Reduction Act were partly driven by movement pressure. But no major new international climate deal emerged, post-COVID momentum stalled significantly, fossil fuel production increased globally 2023-2024.

progress_markers.event: IPCC 1.5°C Special Report published October 2018 — 12-year deadline for action enters mainstream
Greta Thunberg's solo strike outside Swedish parliament August 20 2018 — 'Skolstrejk for klimatet' sign
Greta's COP24 Katowice speech goes viral; movement spreads internationally via social media
March 15 2019: first global student climate strike — 1.4 million students in 100+ countries
May 2019: climate emergency declarations spread — UK parliament first national parliament to declare
September 20 2019: 6 million people globally in climate strikes — largest climate mobilization in history
November 2019: European Green Deal announced by new von der Leyen Commission — partly credited to FFF pressure
COP25 Madrid: movement pressure visible but no major new commitments; coal and oil nations block progress
COVID-19 ends mass street mobilization; online protests have fraction of impact; movement loses momentum
COP26 Glasgow: 1.5°C commitment formalized, coal phase-out agreed in principle; fossil fuel subsidies continue
US Inflation Reduction Act passes — $369B in climate spending, largest US climate investment in history
Ukraine war triggers European gas/coal revival; energy security trumps climate in political discourse
COP28 Dubai hosted by UAE oil exec Sultan Al Jaber; 'phaseout' of fossil fuels watered to 'transition away from'
FFF continues strikes at reduced scale; Greta pivots to Gaza solidarity; movement aging into political mainstream

lead_orgs.name: Fridays for Future (FFF)
lead_orgs.description: Decentralized global network of youth climate strikers; no central organization by design

lead_orgs.name: 350.org
lead_orgs.description: Pre-existing climate org that provided infrastructure and amplified FFF; divestment campaign parallel track

lead_orgs.name: Sunrise Movement (US)
lead_orgs.description: US youth climate political organization; connected FFF energy to Green New Deal policy demands

funders: FFF deliberately unfunded/grassroots to maintain independence
350.org: Rockefeller Brothers Fund, European Climate Foundation
celebrities: Jane Fonda (Fire Drill Fridays — arrested at US Capitol)
government_champions: Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-NY) — Green New Deal
EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen

villains: Saudi Aramco, ADNOC (blocked COP28 phaseout language)

international: UN Secretary-General Guterres (explicitly credited youth movements)

what_actually_changed: FFF normalized youth as a political actor on climate, accelerated climate emergency declarations in 2,000+ jurisdictions, and contributed to the European Green Deal and US Inflation Reduction Act — substantive policy changes. The framing of climate action as intergenerational justice has permanently shifted media discourse. However, global fossil fuel production grew through 2023, emissions hit record highs, and no binding treaty emerged from the movement's peak years. The global strike format lost effectiveness post-COVID and hasn't recovered its 2019 scale.

name: Standing Rock / NoDAPL
outcome_summary: Won a short-term halt under Obama but the pipeline was approved by Trump 4 days into his presidency and has been operational since 2017. The movement produced lasting cultural effects — water is life became a global indigenous rights slogan, land back movement accelerated, indigenous-environmental alliances deepened — but failed in its primary material objective. Pipeline still flows.

progress_markers.event: Iowa farmers and Meskwaki Nation file first objections to DAPL route; legal fights begin
Army Corps routes pipeline near Standing Rock after Bismarck objects to original route — tribe learns of rerouting
Standing Rock Sioux file lawsuit; Sacred Stone Camp established April 2016 by LaDonna Brave Bull Allard
September 3-4 2016: private security dogs attack water protectors on camera; civil rights era resonance
October-November 2016: 300+ tribal nations gather — largest indigenous gathering in a century
November 2016: military veterans stand with Standing Rock; shields imagery goes globally viral
December 4 2016: Obama administration halts pipeline pending Environmental Impact Statement review
January 24 2017: Trump signs executive order approving Dakota Access Pipeline — 4 days into presidency
February 2017: protectors forcibly evicted from Oceti Sakowin camp; arrests; pipeline construction continues
April 2017: DAPL becomes operational; oil flowing under Missouri River
DC federal court orders EIS conducted; pipeline temporarily ordered shut down July 2020
DC Circuit Court reinstates pipeline; Biden administration does not stop it despite campaign promises
Keystone XL pipeline cancelled (Jan 2021 Biden executive order) — NoDAPL energy contributed to this win
Land back movement and indigenous rights consciousness significantly elevated; tribal consultation requirements strengthened in some states
DAPL still operational; Standing Rock Sioux water rights case continues; pipeline has had several oil spills

lead_orgs.name: Standing Rock Sioux Tribe
lead_orgs.description: Lead plaintiff in legal challenges; hosted the camps; Chairman David Archambault II was face of movement

lead_orgs.name: Indigenous Environmental Network
lead_orgs.description: Helped establish Sacred Stone Camp; national indigenous environmental advocacy infrastructure

lead_orgs.name: Earthjustice
lead_orgs.description: Legal arm providing pro bono litigation on pipeline and water rights challenges

villains: TigerSwan (private security firm that surveilled protesters)
allies: 350 cities that passed divestment from DAPL-financing banks
17 banks that eventually pulled DAPL financing after pressure campaigns

international: UN Special Rapporteur on Rights of Indigenous Peoples visited and criticized US response
UNDRIP (UN Declaration on Rights of Indigenous Peoples) cited in legal challenges

what_actually_changed: The Dakota Access Pipeline has been operational since April 2017 and the Standing Rock Sioux's primary demand was not achieved. However, the movement permanently elevated the land back movement, made 'Mni Wiconi' (water is life) a global slogan, accelerated indigenous-environmental alliance-building, and contributed to Keystone XL's cancellation. The Biden administration's failure to stop DAPL despite promises is a key lesson: electoral victories don't automatically translate to movement wins. Court challenges continue but the pipeline keeps flowing.

name: Marriage Equality — The Long Arc
outcome_summary: Full legal victory at the Supreme Court in 2015, sustained by Respect for Marriage Act in 2022. Public support went from 27% (1996) to 70%+ (2024). Gold standard for deliberate narrative engineering producing cultural tipping before legal tipping. Currently vulnerable to SCOTUS reversal if Obergefell is reconsidered, but the 2022 federal statute provides additional protection.

progress_markers.event: Hawaii Supreme Court rules ban on same-sex marriage may be discriminatory — first legal opening; triggers national backlash
DOMA (Defense of Marriage Act) signed by Clinton; federal definition of marriage as one man, one woman
Vermont creates civil unions — first legal recognition; 'separate but equal' compromise
Massachusetts becomes first US state to legalize same-sex marriage (Goodridge v. Dept of Public Health)
11 state constitutional amendments banning same-sex marriage pass on Election Day — Karl Rove uses as mobilization tool
California Prop 8 passes (52-48), banning same-sex marriage after brief legalization — $100M campaign on both sides
Public support crosses 50% majority for first time; New York legalizes same-sex marriage
Obama becomes first sitting president to support marriage equality; 3 states approve at ballot
SCOTUS Windsor decision strikes down DOMA; Prop 8 dismissed on standing; California marriages resume
Obergefell v. Hodges (June 26 2015): SCOTUS rules nationwide marriage equality in 5-4 decision
Respect for Marriage Act passes Congress with bipartisan support; provides federal backup if Obergefell overturned
SCOTUS justices Thomas and Alito signal openness to reconsidering Obergefell; movement remains vigilant on trans rights rollbacks

lead_orgs.name: Freedom to Marry
lead_orgs.description: Founded by Evan Wolfson; strategic 'win in states, win nationally' campaign; dissolved after Obergefell as mission achieved

lead_orgs.name: Lambda Legal
lead_orgs.description: Legal organization that litigated key cases including Goodridge and federal challenges

lead_orgs.name: Human Rights Campaign (HRC)
lead_orgs.description: Largest LGBTQ advocacy org; grassroots and corporate pressure campaigns; $50M+ annual budget

funders: Tim Gill (Gill Foundation — $500M+ to LGBTQ causes)
celebrities: Ellen DeGeneres (came out 1997 — pivotal cultural moment)

allies: Apple, Google, Facebook, Microsoft, Amazon (all filed amicus briefs in Obergefell)

international: UN Human Rights Council (LGBTQ rights resolutions)

what_actually_changed: Same-sex couples can marry in all 50 US states, access federal marriage benefits, and are protected by the Respect for Marriage Act even if Obergefell is reconsidered. Public support went from 27% to 70%+ in three decades — one of the fastest opinion shifts in polling history. The movement demonstrated that a 30-year strategic campaign can transform majoritarian opinion from opposition to support. The remaining gap: marriage equality has not translated to full non-discrimination protections in all states, and trans rights have faced significant legislative rollback since 2021.

name: Chile Estallido Social
outcome_summary: A historic constitutional process was launched but failed twice — both new constitutions rejected by referendum. Gabriel Boric elected president. The 1980 Pinochet constitution remains in effect. Minor social concessions. Political landscape permanently altered but the neoliberal structural model — privatized water, health, education, pensions — not dismantled.

progress_markers.event: 'Penguin Revolution' — high school students protest education system; first major youth uprising of this generation
Massive student protests for free public education; Gabriel Boric and Camila Vallejo emerge as student leaders
No+AFP (anti-privatized pension) movement organizes; demonstrates organizational capacity
Student 'evasion' fare-jump protests grow for weeks; CPI shows mounting inequality; multiple sectors simultaneously aggrieved
October 18 2019: subway fare increase of 30 pesos ($0.04); state of emergency; military in streets for first time since Pinochet
October 25: one million people march in Santiago — largest demonstration in Chilean history
Las Tesis feminist collective performs 'Un Violador en tu Camino' — goes globally viral; performed in 50+ countries
Police violence: 7,000+ injured, 2,500 arrested, 30+ killed, hundreds with eye injuries from rubber bullets
November 15: 'Acuerdo por la Paz Social y la Nueva Constitucion' — agreement for constitutional referendum
October 25 2020 plebiscite: 78% vote for new constitution; 79% vote for elected constitutional convention
Gabriel Boric elected president November 2021 — former student leader, youngest president in Chilean history
September 4 2022: first new constitution rejected in referendum — 62% against, 38% for
December 17 2023: second constitutional draft rejected — 55% against; process ends; Pinochet constitution remains
Boric government continues reform efforts within existing constitution; movement energy dissipated without constitutional victory

lead_orgs.name: No+AFP Movement
lead_orgs.description: Anti-privatized pension movement that built organizing infrastructure before 2019

lead_orgs.name: Las Tesis (feminist collective)
lead_orgs.description: Valparaiso-based feminist collective who created 'Un Violador en tu Camino' — performed in 50+ countries

lead_orgs.name: Coordinadora Feminista 8M
lead_orgs.description: Feminist coalition central to movement identity; organized March 8 strikes

funders: Some funding from left political parties
celebrities: Mon Laferte (musician — exposed breast at VMAs in protest)
government_champions: Elisa Loncon (Mapuche leader, head of constitutional convention)

villains: Enel (electricity — rate rises contributed to grievances)

international: Inter-American Commission on Human Rights monitored abuses

what_actually_changed: The 1980 Pinochet constitution remains in effect after two failed referendum processes — the movement's core failure. However, Chile elected its youngest president in history (Boric, a movement figure), demonstrated that mass mobilization can force constitutional conversations previously unthinkable, and produced 'Un Violador en tu Camino' as a globally replicated feminist tool. Minor social policy improvements occurred but the structural neoliberal model has not been dismantled. The political landscape is permanently altered; the system is not.

name: EndSARS Nigeria
outcome_summary: SARS officially disbanded within 8 days — a nominal victory. But SWAT replaced it with similar practices, the Lekki Toll Gate massacre killed an unknown number of protesters, no soldiers were held accountable, and structural police brutality continues. Most durable legacy: Nigerian youth political awakening that shaped 2023 elections.

progress_markers.event: SARS established; begins targeting young men for extortion and 'looking criminal' (laptops, dreadlocks)
#EndSARS hashtag first appears; Segun Awosanya begins systematic documentation of abuses
First government promise to reform SARS — ignored; pattern repeats in 2019
COVID creates unemployed youth with nothing to lose; SARS abuses accelerate during lockdowns
October 3 2020: video of SARS killing young man in Delta State goes viral on Twitter within hours
October 8-11: spontaneous nationwide protests; no central organization; Twitter coordination via #EndSARS
October 11: Nigerian Police Force officially disbands SARS nationwide — fastest capitulation in recent Nigerian history
Feminist Coalition raises 150M+ naira ($400K) in 72 hours via crowdfunding for protest logistics and injured protesters
October 14: SWAT announced as SARS replacement — widely seen as rebranding without reform
October 20: Lekki Toll Gate massacre — military opens fire on peaceful protesters; 12-60+ killed
October 21: nationwide curfew; movement suppressed by force; organizers arrested
Lagos judicial panel confirms Lekki killings; government ordered to pay compensation — largely unpaid
Peter Obi / Labour Party surge in presidential election driven by EndSARS generation — political awakening affects electoral landscape

lead_orgs.name: Feminist Coalition
lead_orgs.description: Anonymous group of Nigerian women who provided financial infrastructure — raised $400K for protesters in 72 hours

lead_orgs.name: Paradigm Initiative
lead_orgs.description: Digital rights organization that documented police abuses and provided tech infrastructure

lead_orgs.name: Amnesty International Nigeria
lead_orgs.description: Documented 56 deaths during protests; provided international human rights accountability pressure

funders: Crowdfunded $400K via Feminist Coalition (bitcoin and bank transfers)
government_champions: Peter Obi emerged post-EndSARS as political beneficiary rather than champion during protests

allies: Twitter CEO Jack Dorsey (actively supported, enabled Bitcoin donations)

international: US State Department (called for peaceful response)

what_actually_changed: SARS was officially disbanded — a nominal victory. But SWAT replicated SARS practices, no officers were held accountable for Lekki, and structural police brutality in Nigeria has not materially changed. The most durable legacy is political: a generation of Nigerian youth who organized outside traditional party structures developed digital activism infrastructure and in 2023 nearly elected Peter Obi as an outsider presidential candidate through the same organizational methods. The Feminist Coalition model of anonymous, tech-enabled movement finance is a lasting innovation.

name: India Farmers' Protest
outcome_summary: Complete victory: PM Modi announced repeal of all three Farm Laws on national television November 19, 2021 — 13 months into the protest. One of the most significant democratic reversals in Indian history. However, the MSP guarantee was promised but not legislated. A 2024 follow-up protest demanding legal MSP confirms the secondary demand remains unfulfilled.

progress_markers.event: Farmer debt crisis deepens; 300,000+ farmer suicides documented 1995-2020; structural agrarian crisis established
September 2020: Three Farm Acts passed by Parliament in 24 hours with no public consultation — shock to farming communities
October-November 2020: Punjab farmers organize; tractor rallies; state-level mobilization builds
November 26-27 2020: 'Dilli Chalo' — farmers march on Delhi; police use tear gas and water cannons; farmers block highways
December 2020: Samyukt Kisan Morcha coalition of 40+ unions forms with unified demands
January 26 2021: Tractor parade turns chaotic; some protesters breach Red Fort; government uses incident to delegitimize movement
February 2021: Rihanna and Greta tweet support; Indian government's nationalist counter-response amplifies movement globally
Internet shutdowns at protest sites; sedition charges filed against journalist Disha Ravi for sharing toolkit
October 2021: Lakhimpur Kheri violence — BJP minister's son allegedly runs over farmers; 4 killed
November 19 2021: PM Modi announces repeal of all three Farm Acts on national TV — unprecedented capitulation
December 2021: farmers end 13-month sit-in after government commits to MSP committee
Punjab state elections: BJP loses; AAP sweeps — movement's political effect visible
Second farmers' protest begins February 2024 demanding legal MSP guarantee; blocked at Shambhu border

lead_orgs.name: Samyukt Kisan Morcha (SKM)
lead_orgs.description: Coalition of 40+ farmer unions; provided unified platform and discipline across 13-month sit-in

lead_orgs.name: Bharatiya Kisan Union (BKU)
lead_orgs.description: Largest farmer union; Rakesh Tikait's emotional breakdown on national TV reinvigorated movement January 2021

lead_orgs.name: All India Kisan Sabha (CPIM)
lead_orgs.description: Communist-affiliated farmer organization; provided left organizational infrastructure

funders: Sikh diaspora (UK, Canada, US) sent millions in food/supply support
Gurdwaras in Punjab provided food and logistics
celebrities: Rihanna (tweet: 'Why aren't we talking about this?' — went global)
hundreds of Indian musicians and athletes

villains: Adani Group (accused of being primary beneficiary of Farm Acts)
allies: Few — movement was explicitly anti-corporate deregulation

international: Canadian PM Trudeau (expressed support — India recalled ambassador)
UN Special Rapporteurs on food and freedom of assembly

what_actually_changed: All three Farm Acts were repealed in November 2021 — a complete and historic victory on the primary demand. The laws literally ceased to exist, and the government that passed them publicly capitulated. However, the MSP guarantee (minimum support price legally mandated) was promised in committee form but never legislated, meaning farmers' income security remains structurally vulnerable. The 2024 follow-up protest demonstrates the incomplete nature of the win. The movement proved that 13 months of disciplined physical presence can overcome one of the most popular governments in Indian history.

name: South Korea Candlelight Revolution
outcome_summary: Complete success: President Park Geun-hye impeached by parliament December 2016, upheld by Constitutional Court March 2017, arrested and convicted, Moon Jae-in elected. The movement achieved every goal through peaceful democratic means. One of the cleanest examples of mass nonviolent democratic accountability in modern history. The 2024 Yoon Suk-yeol martial law episode showed the model still works.

progress_markers.event: Sewol ferry disaster kills 304 mostly students; government response delayed and dishonest; trust in Park collapses
Park approval ratings decline; corruption allegations accumulate; 'blacklist' of critical artists exposed
'Hell Joseon' discourse peaks among youth; economic discontent; democratic backsliding perceived
October 24-29: JTBC broadcaster reveals Choi Soon-sil possessed and edited classified presidential speeches and policy documents
October 29: First candlelight vigil — 30,000 people in Gwanghwamun Square
November 5: Second vigil — 200,000 people; multigenerational, peaceful, families with children
November 12: Third vigil — 1 million in Seoul, 2 million nationwide; largest protest since democratization
November 26: 1.9 million nationwide; Park approval rating hits 4%
December 9: National Assembly votes to impeach Park 234-56; suspended from duties
March 10: Constitutional Court upholds impeachment unanimously 8-0; Park removed from office
April: Park arrested on corruption and abuse of power charges
May 9: Moon Jae-in elected president — first liberal president in 10 years
Park convicted and sentenced to 25 years (later reduced); Samsung's Lee Jae-yong also convicted
President Yoon declares martial law December 3 2024; rapidly reversed; National Assembly impeaches Yoon December 14 — model works again

lead_orgs.name: Citizens' Action Emergency Committee for Resignation
lead_orgs.description: Coalition of civic organizations coordinating the candlelight vigils

lead_orgs.name: Korean Confederation of Trade Unions (KCTU)
lead_orgs.description: Labor unions provided organizational backbone for rallies

lead_orgs.name: Opposition parties (Democratic Party, People's Party)
lead_orgs.description: Supported impeachment vote in National Assembly; institutional pathway

celebrities: JTBC (broadcaster) — Sohn Suk-hee's investigative journalism was the catalyst
government_champions: National Assembly majority voted to impeach

villains: Samsung (Lee Jae-yong convicted in Choi scandal — bribery of Park administration)
allies: JTBC (independent broadcaster whose investigative reporting triggered the crisis)

international: Minimal international involvement — domestic democratic process worked as designed

what_actually_changed: President Park was removed from office, convicted, and imprisoned. The constitutional process worked as designed. Samsung's Lee Jae-yong was convicted of bribery and served time (later pardoned — ongoing controversy). The 2024 Yoon martial law episode confirmed the model is durable: civil society and parliament rapidly removed an overreaching executive again. South Korea demonstrated that a peaceful, aesthetically compelling mass mobilization can hold even the most powerful president accountable through legal democratic mechanisms — and that the model can be replicated within the same political system less than a decade later.

name: Argentina Abortion / Green Wave
outcome_summary: Full legislative victory: Argentina legalized abortion on demand up to 14 weeks (Law 27,610) on December 30, 2020, entering force January 14, 2021. The Green Wave visual template spread across Latin America and directly influenced Mexico (2021), Colombia (2022). Law remains in effect despite Milei opposition.

progress_markers.event: NiUnaMenos (June 3 2015) establishes feminist coalition infrastructure; organizational base for abortion campaign
Campana Nacional por el Derecho al Aborto Legal forms; annual bill presentations begin; green handkerchief adopted as symbol
First abortion bill reaches Senate floor: 'Green tide' — massive street mobilization; Senate votes June-August 2018
August 9 2018: Senate rejects bill 38-31; protesters weep but immediately recommit — defeat transformed into momentum
Alberto Fernandez elected president on platform including abortion rights — political pathway opens
November 2020: Fernandez government submits abortion bill to parliament with presidential support
December 11 2020: Chamber of Deputies passes 131-117; green handkerchiefs wave in the rain outside parliament
December 30 2020: Senate passes 38-29; Law 27,610 signed — abortion legal to 14 weeks
September 2021: Mexico Supreme Court rules unanimously that criminalization of abortion is unconstitutional — Green Wave direct influence
February 2022: Colombia Constitutional Court legalizes abortion up to 24 weeks — Green Wave influence cited
US Dobbs decision overturning Roe v. Wade creates global backlash; Green Wave becomes symbol of resistance
Javier Milei elected president November 2023; vows to roll back abortion law; has not succeeded legislatively
Law remains in effect; implementation uneven in conservative provinces; regional movement continues expanding

lead_orgs.name: Campana Nacional por el Derecho al Aborto Legal
lead_orgs.description: National coalition that submitted bills annually for years; professional activist infrastructure built over a decade

lead_orgs.name: Socorristas en Red
lead_orgs.description: Clandestine abortion accompaniment network; provided safe abortions before legalization; ensured implementation knowledge

lead_orgs.name: UNFPA Argentina
lead_orgs.description: UN agency that supported legal framework and implementation

allies: Medical associations eventually supported access to legal abortion services

what_actually_changed: Argentina legalized abortion up to 14 weeks of pregnancy — a complete and binding legislative victory. The law is in effect and funded through the public health system (though implementation varies by province due to conscientious objector provisions). The visual template — green handkerchief, 'panuelezo' mass wave — became the signal for feminist movements from Mexico to the Philippines. Mexico decriminalized at the federal level, Colombia expanded access to 24 weeks. The Milei government opposes the law but has not been able to repeal it.

name: NiUnaMenos — Anti-Femicide
outcome_summary: Created the framework for femicide as a named systemic crime. Influenced legislation across Latin America. Generated global feminist organizing language. Built the infrastructure that won the Green Wave abortion victory. Femicide rates have not dramatically declined — the awareness and framing win is real; the structural violence continues.

progress_markers.event: Susana Chavez, Mexican poet who coined 'Ni Una Menos,' murdered in Juarez — slogan becomes memorial to its own author
Chiara Paez (14, pregnant) found buried in boyfriend's backyard in Argentina — case galvanizes feminist networks
June 3 2015: First NiUnaMenos march — 200,000+ in Buenos Aires and 80 cities; journalists, artists, activists organized in 10 days
Argentina's 2012 femicide law gains enforcement focus; femicide as distinct crime category enters legal/media vocabulary
Movement spreads: Chile, Mexico, Uruguay, Peru, Bolivia all hold NiUnaMenos marches within weeks
National strike (paro nacional de mujeres) in Argentina October 19 2016 — first women's strike against femicide
International Women's Strike March 8 2017 — NiUnaMenos contributes to global feminist resurgence parallel to MeToo
Mexico NiUnaMenos march after spate of femicides; 'glitter protest' at CNDH — police chief glitter-bombed
Argentine National Femicide Register publishes annual data — making the pattern visible through official statistics
Movement organizational infrastructure directly enables Green Wave abortion law victory
Femicide rates in Argentina: 2020=298, 2021=295, 2022=280 — declining slightly but not dramatically
Movement continues; March 8 marches in Latin America remain some of world's largest feminist mobilizations

lead_orgs.name: Colectivo NiUnaMenos
lead_orgs.description: Original organizing collective of Argentine journalists, artists, and activists; decentralized model from the start

lead_orgs.name: Marea Feminista
lead_orgs.description: Broader feminist coordination body that emerged from NiUnaMenos infrastructure

lead_orgs.name: Mumala (Mujeres de la Matria Latinoamericana)
lead_orgs.description: Published annual femicide registry; turned anecdotal cases into systematized data

funders: UNFPA for data and research support
celebrities: journalists Ingrid Beck and Marcela Ojeda (key organizers)
government_champions: Argentine government adopted femicide registry legislation

villains: Media outlets that initially covered femicides as 'crimen pasional' (crime of passion)
allies: Argentine newspapers that shifted coverage framing after movement pressure

international: UN Special Rapporteur on Violence Against Women
ECLAC (UN Latin American economic commission) — publishes regional femicide data
Inter-American Convention on Violence Against Women (Belem do Para)

what_actually_changed: Femicide is now legally recognized as a distinct crime category across most of Latin America, with enhanced penalties. Official femicide registries exist in multiple countries, making the pattern visible through data. Media coverage of gender-based killings has fundamentally shifted from 'domestic tragedy' to systemic analysis. However, femicide rates have not dramatically declined — between 200-300 women are killed in Argentina alone annually. The movement created the cultural and organizational infrastructure for the Green Wave, its most tangible policy legacy.

name: Fees Must Fall
outcome_summary: Fee-free higher education for students from families earning under R350,000/year introduced in 2018 — a significant policy win. But the 2015 zero fee increase was only a one-year victory; decolonization demands were largely unmet; NSFAS implementation is chronically underfunded; the 'missing middle' problem persists. Leaders faced criminal charges.

progress_markers.event: #RhodesMustFall: UCT students demand Cecil Rhodes statue removal March 2015; statue falls April 9 — confidence boost for student activism
RMF movement spreads to Oxford (Rhodes Must Fall Oxford); decolonization agenda gets international traction
October 14: Wits University announces 10.5% fee increase; student council VP storms administration building
October 23: Thousands march on parliament in Cape Town; police use stun grenades; national attention
October 23: President Zuma announces zero fee increase for 2016 — movement's core immediate demand met
#FeesMustFall 2016 relaunched; demands escalate to zero fees permanently; campus fires and violence
Government deploys private security on campuses; hundreds arrested; movement facing criticism for divisive tactics
December 2017: Zuma announces fee-free higher education for students from families earning under R350,000/year
NSFAS (student loans) expanded to cover more students; implementation begins
NSFAS implementation plagued by administrative failures; many students excluded; 'missing middle' problem remains
Movement leaders face criminal charges; institutional decolonization remains mainly symbolic (curriculum changes, some statue removals)
NSFAS remains underfunded and administratively dysfunctional; free education policy exists but delivery is inconsistent

lead_orgs.name: South African Students' Congress (SASCO)
lead_orgs.description: ANC-aligned student organization; part of movement but sometimes in tension with more radical elements

lead_orgs.name: #RhodesMustFall Movement
lead_orgs.description: Decentralized decolonization movement; intersected with Fees Must Fall organizational infrastructure

lead_orgs.name: Progressive Youth Alliance
lead_orgs.description: Coalition of student organizations that coordinated demands and marches

funders: Grassroots student organizing; no major institutional funders
celebrities: Naledi Chirwa (student leader who later became MP)
government_champions: President Zuma (capitulated on fees — not a champion but responded)

villains: University management associations (initially resisted all demands)

what_actually_changed: Fee-free higher education for students from low-income households is now policy — a genuine change that directly benefits hundreds of thousands of students annually. Some statues removed, some curriculum decolonization. However, the 'missing middle' remains a problem, NSFAS implementation is chronically underfunded and badly administered, and university culture and curriculum remain largely unchanged from apartheid-era patterns. The decolonization agenda is ongoing without institutional resolution.

name: Sudan Revolution
outcome_summary: Al-Bashir's 30-year rule ended April 2019. For approximately 2 years there was a civilian-led transitional government. Then a 2021 military coup reversed the democratic transition. The subsequent SAF-RSF civil war (April 2023) has caused catastrophic humanitarian disaster — 8M+ displaced, world's largest displacement crisis. The revolution succeeded in removing Bashir and led to a civil war.

progress_markers.event: Omar al-Bashir seizes power in military coup; 30-year Islamist authoritarian rule begins
ICC issues arrest warrant for al-Bashir for genocide in Darfur; regime internationally isolated
Bread and fuel prices triple; economy in freefall; Sudanese Professional Association begins organizing
December 19 2018: Protests begin in Atbara after bread price tripling; security kills protesters; spreads to Khartoum
April 6 2019: Massive sit-in begins outside military HQ — city within a city with library, clinic, music stage
April 11 2019: Military removes and arrests al-Bashir — ending 30-year rule
June 3 2019: RSF (Rapid Support Forces) massacres sit-in protesters — 60-128+ killed, hundreds raped, thousands injured
July-August 2019: International pressure secures civilian-military 'Sovereignty Council' power-sharing deal
Abdalla Hamdok (civilian economist) appointed PM; democratic transition begins; ICC cooperation on Bashir
October 25 2021: Military coup by Burhan and Hemeti; Hamdok arrested; democratic transition ended
Hamdok resigned January 2022; protests suppressed; activists arrested and tortured
April 15 2023: SAF-RSF civil war breaks out; Khartoum becomes war zone; 8M+ displaced
Civil war continues; RSF commits atrocities in Darfur; revolution's gains entirely erased by catastrophic conflict

lead_orgs.name: Sudanese Professional Association (SPA)
lead_orgs.description: Coalition of doctors, lawyers, engineers; provided organizational backbone and credibility to protests

lead_orgs.name: Sudan Women's Union
lead_orgs.description: Women estimated 70% of protesters; women's organizations central to movement character

lead_orgs.name: Resistance Committees
lead_orgs.description: Grassroots neighborhood democracy structures that maintained protests through coups and crackdowns

celebrities: Alaa Salah ('Kandaka' — iconic image of woman in white leading protest chants)

villains: RSF/Hemeti (major business empire in gold mining)
Wagner Group (supported RSF in civil war)
allies: Few — international business supported civilian transition mostly symbolically

what_actually_changed: Al-Bashir's 30-year dictatorship ended — a genuine historic achievement. For approximately 2 years Sudan had a civilian-led transitional government pursuing democratic institutions. All of this was reversed by the 2021 coup and then catastrophically destroyed by the 2023 civil war, producing one of the world's worst humanitarian crises. Sudan in 2025 is in a dramatically worse humanitarian and political state than in 2018. The revolution succeeded in removing Bashir and failed to prevent what followed.

name: Malawi Election Protests
outcome_summary: Complete legal and democratic victory: Constitutional Court annulled the fraudulent 2019 election, fresh elections held June 2020, incumbent Mutharika lost, Chakwera inaugurated. First time in African history a court annulled a presidential election and the outcome was peacefully accepted. A landmark continental democratic precedent.

progress_markers.event: Peter Mutharika elected president; corruption allegations and governance concerns begin accumulating
Human Rights Defenders Coalition (HRDC) active; civil society monitoring institutions strengthening
May 21 2019: Elections held; MEC announces Mutharika wins; immediate concerns from observers about irregularities
Evidence emerges: ballot tallying sheets show tipp-ex corrections; photographs circulate on social media — undeniable physical evidence
Chakwera and Chilima challenge election in Constitutional Court; HRDC organizes sustained street protests
Massive sustained protests in Blantyre and Lilongwe; tear gas, clashes — civil society maintains pressure for months
MEC Chairwoman Jane Ansah resigns under pressure — first institutional accountability
February 3 2020: Constitutional Court annuls 2019 election unanimously — landmark ruling citing widespread fraud including tipp-ex alterations
Malawi Electoral Commission reconstituted with new leadership
June 23 2020: Fresh elections held; Lazarus Chakwera wins 58.57% — clean result accepted by all parties
June 28 2020: Mutharika accepts results; Chakwera inaugurated — peaceful democratic transition
SADC election observation praises process; becomes continental precedent for democratic accountability
Chakwera government faces corruption allegations — democratic process intact but governance disappoints

lead_orgs.name: Human Rights Defenders Coalition (HRDC)
lead_orgs.description: Led street protests; maintained sustained pressure; Timothy Mtambo as key spokesperson

lead_orgs.name: Malawi Law Society
lead_orgs.description: Supported legal challenges to election results

lead_orgs.name: Public Affairs Committee (PAC)
lead_orgs.description: Interfaith body that coordinated civic engagement and monitored process

funders: International support for election monitoring (EU, SADC)
US and UK democracy support programs
celebrities: Timothy Mtambo (HRDC leader — civic leader rather than celebrity)
Catholic bishops (vocal supporters of democratic process)
government_champions: Constitutional Court judges (maintained independence under enormous pressure)

allies: Independent media (created space for evidence sharing)

international: SADC (Southern African Development Community) observer mission

what_actually_changed: A fraudulent election was legally annulled, a new election was held, the incumbent lost and accepted the result, and a new president was peacefully inaugurated. The Malawi Constitutional Court ruling is a continental landmark — referenced in subsequent African election disputes. Civil society sustained pressure plus independent judiciary can work even against an incumbent who controls state resources. Long-term: democratic norms strengthened; governance quality of new government is debated but the democratic process was preserved.

name: Zimbabwe — Fall of Mugabe
outcome_summary: Robert Mugabe's 37-year rule ended November 2017. But the removal was primarily a military/elite operation enabled by popular celebration, not a democratic revolution. Emmerson Mnangagwa has continued authoritarian governance. 2023 elections deeply flawed. Zimbabwe traded one ZANU-PF ruler for another. Economic crisis continues.

progress_markers.event: Farm seizures; economic collapse begins; Zimbabwe loses most of its economic value over following decade
Hyperinflation peaks at 89 sextillion percent; Z$100 trillion notes issued; economy completely destroyed
Unity government with MDC briefly stabilizes economy using foreign currencies; collapse averted temporarily
Mugabe, 93, visibly declining; wife Grace Mugabe (G40 faction) positions for succession; military (Lacoste faction) alarmed
November 6: Mugabe sacks VP Mnangagwa; military sees this as existential threat to their interests
November 14-15: Military takes over ZBC broadcaster; announces 'not a coup'; places Mugabe under house arrest
November 18: 100,000+ march in Harare celebrating military action; war veterans and civil society join
November 21: Mugabe resigns after parliament begins impeachment proceedings; nation celebrates
July 2018 elections: Mnangagwa wins narrowly; post-election protests met with military force; 6 killed
January 2019 fuel price protests; military cracks down; internet shut down; 12+ killed
COVID used as pretext to arrest journalists and opposition figures; Hopewell Chin'ono arrested
August 2023: Elections widely condemned as fraudulent; Mnangagwa declared winner; SADC observers cite irregularities
Economic crisis continues; brain drain; authoritarian rule entrenched; no meaningful democratic progress

lead_orgs.name: Zimbabwe National Army / ZDF
lead_orgs.description: Primary actor in Mugabe removal — military operation that enabled popular celebration

lead_orgs.name: Zimbabwe National War Veterans Association
lead_orgs.description: War veterans turned against Mugabe; gave military action popular legitimacy

lead_orgs.name: MDC (Movement for Democratic Change)
lead_orgs.description: Long-standing opposition; not the primary force of removal; benefited temporarily

funders: Military self-funded; no movement funding structure
celebrities: Grace Mugabe (villain; her ambition triggered the coup)
government_champions: SADC and AU gave the transition regional legitimacy after the fact

what_actually_changed: Robert Mugabe's 37-year personal rule ended — a genuine historic milestone. However, ZANU-PF remains in power under Mnangagwa, who has continued authoritarian methods including election manipulation, press suppression, and using security forces against protesters. The economic model has not changed. There is no meaningful democratic progress. What changed: one old man left power. What didn't change: the system that sustained him.

name: Green Belt Movement — Kenya
outcome_summary: 51 million trees planted, constitutional environmental rights secured, model replicated in 30+ countries, Nobel Peace Prize 2004. A 27-year movement built from zero that genuinely changed Kenya's landscape and governance and created an enduring international institution. One of the most complete grassroots environmental-democracy victories of the 20th century. Continues under new leadership.

progress_markers.event: Wangari Maathai founds Green Belt Movement; organizes rural women to plant trees; links environment to democracy and women's empowerment
Maathai challenges Moi regime; GBM declared subversive; organizers harassed and monitored
Maathai blocks Uhuru Park skyscraper development — trees saved; Moi government retreats under international pressure
Maathai beaten and arrested during protests for multiparty democracy; jailed briefly; movement continues
Pro-democracy hunger strike in Uhuru Park by mothers of political prisoners; succeeded in pressuring releases
30,000 women organized; 20 million trees planted; model being replicated internationally across Africa
End of Moi era; multi-party democracy restored; political space for movement expands dramatically
Wangari Maathai wins Nobel Peace Prize — first environmentalist, first African woman to receive Nobel Peace Prize
Maathai appointed Assistant Minister for Environment; 40 million trees planted; movement inside government
New Kenyan constitution includes environmental rights — movement's advocacy contributed to constitutional provision
Maathai dies September 25 2011; Green Belt Movement continues as permanent institution
51+ million trees planted since 1977; movement operating in 30+ African countries
Kenya's newly elected Ruto government announces massive tree-planting program — GBM legacy influences national policy

lead_orgs.name: Green Belt Movement
lead_orgs.description: Founded 1977; permanent institution; operates community tree nurseries and environmental democracy advocacy across Africa

lead_orgs.name: National Council of Women of Kenya (NCWK)
lead_orgs.description: Umbrella body through which GBM was initially launched

lead_orgs.name: UNEP
lead_orgs.description: Headquartered in Nairobi; supported GBM; Maathai was active in UNEP governance

celebrities: Wangari Maathai herself — Nobel laureate was the ambassador
government_champions: Wangari Maathai as Assistant Minister for Environment

villains: Moi regime business allies (real estate developers threatening Uhuru Park)

what_actually_changed: 51+ million trees planted — a genuine, verifiable, physical change to Kenya's landscape. Environmental rights in the 2010 Kenyan constitution. An enduring institution (GBM) that has operated continuously for 47 years. The Nobel Prize validated the model that environmental work, political corruption, and women's empowerment are a unified justice framework — a conceptual contribution that influenced environmental movements globally. Women in 30+ African countries organized through GBM-inspired models.

name: Kenya Maandamano / Finance Bill Protests 2024
outcome_summary: Finance Bill 2024 fully withdrawn by President Ruto within 8 days — a rapid and complete capitulation on the primary demand. Cabinet reshuffled. Represents Gen Z's first major political victory via TikTok-organized movement in Africa — a new model of decentralized digital organizing with real-world policy consequences.

progress_markers.event: Ruto elected president on 'hustler economy' pro-poor platform; expectations of economic relief
Cost of living crisis deepens; Finance Bill 2023 includes harsh tax measures; smaller protests; precedent set
Finance Bill 2024 proposed: cooking oil tax, bread tax, mobile money tax — hitting daily survival costs
June 18 2024: Gen Z organizers call protest via TikTok; larger than expected; police use live ammunition — 3 killed, dozens wounded
June 25: Protesters breach parliament; parliament set alight; 13 killed overall in protest week; global media attention
June 26: President Ruto signs withdrawal of Finance Bill — complete reversal of flagship tax policy
July: Ruto dismisses most of his cabinet; announces national dialogue and new government formation
Ruto invites opposition into government; broad-based government formed — criticized as co-optation by some protesters
Subsequent protest demands (Ruto resignation, accountability) not met; some protest leaders arrested
Movement continues pressing accountability demands; new Finance Bill passed with fewer controversial measures

lead_orgs.name: Decentralized Gen Z Network (no formal organization)
lead_orgs.description: Protests organized via TikTok and X; deliberately leaderless to prevent co-optation or targeted arrests

lead_orgs.name: Okoa Uchumi (Save the Economy)
lead_orgs.description: Civil society coalition that amplified demands and provided some organizational structure

lead_orgs.name: Law Society of Kenya
lead_orgs.description: Filed legal challenges and supported protesters' rights

funders: Essentially self-funded through digital small donations
celebrities: No single champion — decentralized identity was strategic
government_champions: None initially — government was the target

villains: Ruto government financiers whose tax relief was embedded in the Finance Bill
allies: Independent Kenyan media provided live coverage

international: UN Human Rights Office called for investigation

what_actually_changed: The Finance Bill 2024 was withdrawn — complete reversal of flagship government policy within 8 days. Ruto's cabinet was dismissed and reconstructed. The movement demonstrated that TikTok-era organizing, intentionally leaderless, can win material policy victories against sitting governments. Finance bill withdrawal saved Kenyan households from significant additional tax burden. Long-term structural change to Kenya's fiscal governance remains incomplete, but the precedent of rapid capitulation to youth protest pressure is established.

name: Burkina Faso Popular Uprising
outcome_summary: Compaoré's 27-year rule ended in 24 hours. A civilian government was elected and governed for 7 years. But by 2025 Burkina Faso is under military rule aligned with Russia, experiencing one of the world's worst jihadist insurgencies, with press suppressed and democracy suspended. The uprising succeeded in removing a dictator and failed entirely to build institutional foundations for democracy.

progress_markers.event: Blaise Compaore seizes power by assassinating Thomas Sankara; begins 27-year rule
Arab Spring inspires protests in Burkina Faso; Compaore makes concessions but maintains power
Balai Citoyen (Citizen's Broom) civil society movement founded by Smockey (rapper) and Serge Bambara; organizes resistance to constitutional amendment
October 30: Parliament session to vote on constitutional amendment; thousands storm parliament; building set on fire; soldiers pull back
October 31: Compaore flees to Ivory Coast within 24 hours; military announces transitional authority
Presidential guard (RSP) attempts coup September 2015; defeated; RSP dissolved
November 2015: Transition elections; Roch Marc Christian Kabore elected president — civilian government established
Jihadist attacks begin in north; Kabore government unable to control deteriorating security situation
Security catastrophically deteriorates; millions displaced; jihadists control large swaths of territory
January 24 2022: Military coup — Kabore removed; Lt. Col. Damiba takes power
September 30 2022: Second military coup — Capt. Ibrahim Traore removes Damiba
Traore government expels French forces; invites Wagner Group/Russia; suspends civilian press freedom
40%+ of territory under jihadist control; 2M+ displaced; democracy suspended; 2014 revolution's gains entirely erased

lead_orgs.name: Balai Citoyen (Citizen's Broom)
lead_orgs.description: Civil society movement founded 2013 by Smockey (rapper) and Serge Bambara; organized anti-Compaore resistance

lead_orgs.name: Confederation Generale du Travail du Burkina (CGTB)
lead_orgs.description: Trade union confederation that coordinated with civil society during uprising

lead_orgs.name: Burkina Faso Bar Association and professional associations
lead_orgs.description: Lent legitimacy to anti-Compaore campaign

funders: Grassroots; no major external funders disclosed
government_champions: ECOWAS recognized transition; transitional authorities filled vacuum after Compaore fled
international: ECOWAS (recognized transition; later imposed sanctions after 2022 coups)
African Union (suspended Burkina Faso after 2022 coups)

what_actually_changed: Compaore's 27-year rule ended in a single day. A civilian government was elected and governed for 7 years. This was the entire positive legacy. By 2025, Burkina Faso is under military rule, aligned with Russia, experiencing one of the world's worst jihadist insurgencies, with press suppressed and democracy suspended. The 2014 uprising succeeded in removing a dictator and failed entirely to build institutional foundations for democracy that could survive security crisis. A textbook case of revolutionary success without institutional consolidation.

name: Ethiopia Oromo Protests / Abiy Ahmed Rise
outcome_summary: Protests forced sweeping reforms and produced Africa's youngest Nobel Peace Prize winner as PM. Abiy Ahmed released political prisoners, liberalized media, and negotiated Eritrea peace. However, the Tigray war (2020-2022) caused 500,000+ deaths. Oromo marginalization continues; ethnic federalism remains contested. Reform was real but partial.

progress_markers.event: 'Addis Ababa Integrated Development Master Plan' threatens Oromo farmland absorption without compensation
November 2015: student protests against master plan; security forces kill protesters; protests escalate across Oromia
Master Plan officially abandoned January 2016 — first major concession to protesters
October 2 2016: Irreecha festival massacre — security fires on crowd; 52+ killed in stampede and shooting
Irreecha massacre triggers nationwide uprising; international media; EPRDF under maximum pressure
State of emergency declared October 2016; internet shutdowns; mass arrests; 1,000+ detained
February 2018: PM Hailemariam Desalegn resigns — extraordinary concession to protest pressure
April 2018: Abiy Ahmed (Oromo reformer) becomes PM; releases 60,000 political prisoners; lifts state of emergency
October 2019: Abiy Ahmed wins Nobel Peace Prize for Eritrea peace deal
June 2020: murder of musician Hachalu Hundessa triggers Oromo protests; 239 killed in crackdown
November 2020: Tigray war begins; Abiy orders military offensive; atrocities documented by UN
Pretoria Agreement ends Tigray war November 2022; 500,000+ dead; Abiy's human rights record destroyed
Amhara conflict continues; Oromo Liberation Army active; ethnic tensions unresolved; Abiy governing increasingly autocratically

lead_orgs.name: Qeerroo (Oromo Youth Movement)
lead_orgs.description: Decentralized Oromo youth network that sustained protests; operated with disciplined non-violence

lead_orgs.name: Macha-Tulama Self-Help Association
lead_orgs.description: Oromo advocacy organization; provided institutional backing before being disbanded

lead_orgs.name: Oromo Liberation Front (OLF)
lead_orgs.description: Armed movement that returned from exile under Abiy; later re-designated terrorist organization

funders: Ethiopian diaspora (US, Europe, Australia) significant funder
No major institutional funders — regime suppressed organized civil society
celebrities: Hachalu Hundessa (murdered musician whose death triggered 2020 Oromo protests — 239 killed)
government_champions: Abiy Ahmed (former activist turned PM — complicated legacy)
international: African Union (headquarters in Addis; complicated role in Tigray war)
US State Department (oscillated between engagement and pressure)
EU (suspended some aid during Tigray war)
Nobel Committee (2019 prize — ultimately embarrassing given Tigray war)

what_actually_changed: The EPRDF coalition that dominated Ethiopia for 27 years was transformed under protest pressure: Abiy Ahmed's reforms — releasing political prisoners, allowing diaspora opposition return, liberalizing media, making peace with Eritrea — were genuinely historic. But the Tigray war revealed these reforms as partial, and Abiy's government has been accused of serious human rights violations. Oromo land rights and political representation remain contested. The movement succeeded in changing who held power; it did not succeed in building accountable democratic institutions.

name: Ghana #FixTheCountry
outcome_summary: Limited structural change — protests occurred after successful challenge to police injunction, government announced some infrastructure investment, no major policy change. Primarily an awareness and discourse-shifting movement. Contributed to political climate that saw Mahama defeat Bawumia in December 2024 elections.

progress_markers.event: COVID exposes infrastructure gaps in Ghana; fuel prices rise; cost of living crisis develops
#FixTheCountry begins as social media commentary on infrastructure failures, corruption, and poor governance
May 2021: Heavy rains and flooding in Accra kills 6; hashtag trends nationally; protest proposed
Police obtain court injunction blocking protest; civil society challenges it — court battles attract more attention than the flood
Injunction successfully challenged; protests proceed legally — August 4 2021 protest in Accra
Government response: announces road and drainage projects but no structural change in accountability
Movement lacks organizational depth; protests relatively small given hashtag's national prominence
Ghana's economic crisis deepens; IMF bailout required; 'dumsor' (power cuts) return; movement discourse continues
Formal movement energy dissipated; economic crisis continues under Akufo-Addo government
December 2024: John Mahama (NDC) wins presidential election; partly driven by #FixTheCountry sentiment in electorate

lead_orgs.name: #FixTheCountry Core (informal network)
lead_orgs.description: Social media activists without formal organizational structure; Ameyaw Kwesi Koffie prominent

lead_orgs.name: OccupyGhana
lead_orgs.description: Established civil society watchdog that provided some organizational backing

lead_orgs.name: Ghana Bar Association
lead_orgs.description: Challenged police injunction against protest; legal advocacy

funders: Crowdfunded; diaspora support via social media
government_champions: Minority in parliament (NDC opposition) supported right to protest

villains: Government contractors implicated in infrastructure failures

what_actually_changed: No major structural policy change resulted. The court victory over the injunction is a procedural win for protest rights. The movement's primary lasting impact was contributing to a political discourse that held the Akufo-Addo government accountable through the 2024 elections, which NDC won partly on 'fix the economy' messaging. Ghana's economic crisis and infrastructure failures remain ongoing structural problems.

name: Morocco Hirak Rif Movement
outcome_summary: Nine months of sustained protests, some regional investment announcements, then mass trials and long prison sentences for leaders — Nasser Zefzafi sentenced to 20 years. No structural reform of the relationship between the Rif region and the Moroccan state. Movement effectively suppressed through legal prosecution of its leadership.

progress_markers.event: Rif rebellion crushed by King Hassan II; memory of state violence against Berber north embedded in regional identity
Arab Spring: February 20 Movement protests in Morocco; King Mohammed VI offers constitutional reforms widely seen as cosmetic
October 28 2016: Mouhcine Fikri, fish vendor, crushed to death in garbage truck trying to retrieve confiscated swordfish; video goes viral
Thousands protest regional neglect and police impunity in Al Hoceima and Rif cities within days
Sustained protests through winter; Hirak Rif movement formalizes under Nasser Zefzafi; 'Haqquna' (our right) chant sustained
Movement grows to tens of thousands; government announces $12B Rif development plan — inadequately implemented
May 26 2017: Zefzafi arrested after interrupting Friday prayer to address worshippers; mass arrests follow
June 2017: 25,000+ protest in Rabat and Casablanca in solidarity; government does not respond
June 2018: Mass trial; Zefzafi and 5 other leaders sentenced to 20 years; 47 activists total sentenced
Appeals uphold sentences; international criticism from Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch
Zefzafi and core leaders remain imprisoned; some prisoner releases; Rif structural underdevelopment continues
Morocco's 2030 World Cup investment focuses on major cities; Rif marginalization continues structurally

lead_orgs.name: Hirak Rif Movement (informal)
lead_orgs.description: Decentralized protest movement led by Nasser Zefzafi; no formal organization — did not protect against mass trials

lead_orgs.name: Association des droits de l'Homme au Maroc (AMDH)
lead_orgs.description: Moroccan human rights organization that documented arrests and supported detainees

lead_orgs.name: Solidarity committees in diaspora (Europe)
lead_orgs.description: Moroccan diaspora in Netherlands, Belgium, France organized pressure campaigns

funders: International human rights organizations provided documentation support
celebrities: Diaspora musicians and artists expressed solidarity
government_champions: No significant political allies within Moroccan government
international: Amnesty International (named Zefzafi prisoner of conscience)

what_actually_changed: Some regional development investment was announced for the Rif region — inadequately implemented. Movement leaders remain imprisoned on 20-year sentences. No structural reform of Morocco's political system or Berber cultural rights. International pressure has not secured prisoner releases. The Fikri case created significant momentary awareness but without institutional protection — visual power alone could not protect the movement from patient state repression.

name: Mali Protests / IBK Resignation
outcome_summary: President IBK resigned following a military coup on August 18, 2020 — the nominal demand was met, but by the military. Mali has subsequently had two more coups, expelled French forces, invited Wagner Group, withdrawn from ECOWAS, and experienced catastrophic security deterioration. The movement that removed IBK set the conditions for arguably worse governance.

progress_markers.event: Tuareg rebellion and jihadist seizure of northern Mali; French intervention saves the south; state credibility damaged
IBK elected president on anti-corruption platform; promises not delivered; security deteriorates
March 2020 legislative elections marred by fraud; Supreme Court reinstates contested results; June 5 Movement forms
June 5 2020: Massive protests in Bamako; Imam Dicko leads diverse coalition of secular and religious groups
July 10-11: Protesters attack and briefly occupy parliament and state TV — government in crisis
ECOWAS mediates; IBK offers national unity government — protesters reject, demand full resignation
August 18 2020: Military coup removes IBK; CNSP junta takes power; protesters celebrate — military, not civilians, now govern
May 2021: Coup within coup — Colonel Assimi Goita removes transitional civilian president; fully military government
French Operation Barkhane expelled; Wagner Group invited; ECOWAS imposes sanctions
ECOWAS and UN peacekeeping mission (MINUSMA) expelled; Mali isolated internationally
Mali withdraws from ECOWAS along with Burkina Faso and Niger; jihadist insurgency controls large territories
Goita government continues military rule; no civilian transition timeline; Wagner/Russia entrenched

lead_orgs.name: M5-RFP (June 5 Movement — Rally of Patriotic Forces)
lead_orgs.description: Coalition of opposition parties, civil society, and religious leaders; led street protests

lead_orgs.name: High Islamic Council of Mali (HCIM)
lead_orgs.description: Imam Dicko's institutional base; provided religious legitimacy to protests

lead_orgs.name: CMAS (Imam Dicko's direct political vehicle)
lead_orgs.description: Coordination of movements and associations supporting Imam Dicko

celebrities: Imam Mahmoud Dicko (the central figure — Islamic leader turned protest symbol)
government_champions: ECOWAS tried to mediate but failed; AU condemned coup

villains: French state and corporations (accumulated resentment among Malians)
Wagner Group (beneficiary of expulsion of French forces)

what_actually_changed: IBK's government was removed — the stated demand. But democracy was not the outcome. Mali in 2025 is under military rule, aligned with Russia, experiencing a worse jihadist insurgency, isolated from regional institutions, and with no civilian democratic transition in sight. Imam Dicko, who helped lead the movement, became politically marginalized after the coup. The movement's success in removing IBK directly enabled the conditions that led to three successive military governments and regional isolation. Net outcome score: 0.

name: South Africa Marikana — Platinum Belt Massacre
outcome_summary: 34 miners killed by state police in democratic South Africa. Workers received a partial wage increase. No police were criminally convicted. ANC's moral authority among Black working-class South Africans severely damaged. Economic Freedom Fighters founded on the footage. Lonmin closed. Massacre is a lasting symbol of post-apartheid inequality's brutality.

progress_markers.event: Post-apartheid South Africa: racial wage gap persists in mining; rock drillers — most dangerous job — still earning poverty wages
Julius Malema rises in ANC Youth League; begins articulating economic transformation beyond Rainbow Nation narrative
Platinum belt wildcat strikes spread; workers reject NUM as too cozy with ANC; AMCU growing as alternative union
Lonmin workers demand wage increase from R4,000 to R12,500/month; management refuses; strike enters second week
August 16 2012: South African police open fire on striking miners; 34 killed, 78 wounded; filmed from multiple angles
Images of miners being shot while retreating circulate globally — first post-apartheid massacre of Black workers by Black government
Lonmin eventually grants 22% wage increase — partial demand met, far short of R12,500 demand
Cyril Ramaphosa (Lonmin board member, former NUM leader) faces intense public scrutiny for role in calling in police
Julius Malema founds Economic Freedom Fighters (EFF) directly on Marikana platform; wins parliament seats 2014
Farlam Commission report: criticizes police leadership but recommends no criminal prosecutions; effectively a whitewash
National Prosecuting Authority declines to prosecute police officers; no criminal accountability
Lonmin sold to Sibanye-Stillwater; eventually mine closed; workers who survived now unemployed
Marikana Memorial maintained; families still seeking accountability; Ramaphosa presidency shadowed by massacre
Mining sector wages remain inadequate relative to cost of living; AMCU vs NUM rivalry continues; no systemic change

lead_orgs.name: Association of Mineworkers and Construction Union (AMCU)
lead_orgs.description: Breakaway union that represented Marikana workers; grew from 9,000 to 300,000 members post-massacre

lead_orgs.name: Marikana Support Campaign
lead_orgs.description: Civil society coalition that provided legal and moral support to survivors and families

lead_orgs.name: Economic Freedom Fighters (EFF)
lead_orgs.description: Political party founded by Malema on Marikana platform; now major opposition party in South Africa

funders: Marikana families largely self-funded through crowdfunding
Legal aid from civil society organizations
Some solidarity from international mining unions
celebrities: Julius Malema (used Marikana as founding platform for EFF)
government_champions: None — government was complicit; Ramaphosa's role as Lonmin board member made ANC government defensive

villains: Cyril Ramaphosa (board member — 'shoot the bastards' email controversy)
Mining industry broadly for poverty wages
allies: Few — mining sector was largely united against workers' demands

international: UK media (Lonmin was London-listed; extensive British coverage)

what_actually_changed: A partial wage increase was granted. AMCU grew into a major mining union. The EFF was founded and is now a significant parliamentary force. Public consciousness about post-apartheid inequality was permanently sharpened — the myth of post-apartheid as a complete economic transformation was definitively punctured. However, no police officers were held criminally accountable, no systemic change to mining sector wage structure occurred, and Lonmin eventually closed. Ramaphosa became president despite his role — illustrating the limits of moral accountability in South African politics.

name: Liberia Peace Women / Leymah Gbowee
outcome_summary: Charles Taylor was forced from power, a peace deal was signed, Ellen Johnson Sirleaf was elected (first female African head of state), and Leymah Gbowee won the Nobel Peace Prize. One of the clearest cases in modern history of direct nonviolent action by women changing the outcome of a violent conflict. The movement achieved every goal.

progress_markers.event: First Liberian Civil War begins under Charles Taylor; 250,000+ killed over 14 years of intermittent conflict
Second Civil War begins; Taylor's forces committing atrocities; women and children primary victims of sexual violence
Leymah Gbowee organizes Women in Peacebuilding Network (WIPNET); brings Muslim and Christian women together under shared identity as mothers
Women begin prayer vigils in white T-shirts at Monrovia fish market — white = purity + visibility; sex strike announced as pressure tactic
April 2003: 'sex strike' gains international media attention; Taylor summoned women to State House; they hand him a peace petition
Peace talks in Ghana, July 2003: Women's delegation surrounds the building; when talks stall they chain themselves in the hall
Gbowee threatens to invoke traditional 'bad luck' through disrobing — powerful cultural curse when mothers direct it at men; negotiators return to table
August 2003: Taylor agrees to exile in Nigeria; peace deal signed; second civil war ends
November 2005: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf elected president — first female head of state in Africa
Taylor indicted by Special Court for Sierra Leone for war crimes; arrested when he attempts return
Leymah Gbowee wins Nobel Peace Prize (shared with Ellen Johnson Sirleaf and Tawakkol Karman of Yemen)
Charles Taylor convicted by Special Court for Sierra Leone — first sitting head of state convicted by international court since Nuremberg
Ellen Johnson Sirleaf's presidency ends amid corruption allegations; George Weah elected; women's representation drops in government
Liberia remains fragile but peaceful; Women in Peacebuilding Network continues; Gbowee's model replicated globally

lead_orgs.name: Women in Peacebuilding Network (WIPNET)
lead_orgs.description: Leymah Gbowee's organizing vehicle; brought Muslim and Christian women together; logistically organized the movement

lead_orgs.name: Women of Liberia Mass Action for Peace (WLMAP)
lead_orgs.description: Broader coalition that expanded WIPNET's work into mass nonviolent action

lead_orgs.name: Mano River Women's Peace Network
lead_orgs.description: Regional West African women's peace network that provided cross-border solidarity

funders: USAID and US State Department (peacebuilding programs)
Grassroots fundraising by Liberian women themselves
celebrities: Ellen Johnson Sirleaf (Nobel laureate, first female African president)
government_champions: Kofi Annan (UN Secretary-General facilitated Ghana peace talks)
ECOWAS (provided Ghana venue and diplomatic pressure)
Nigerian President Obasanjo (hosted Taylor in exile)

villains: Charles Taylor's timber and diamond interests that funded the war
International logging and mining companies operating during conflict

international: Special Court for Sierra Leone (Taylor prosecution)

what_actually_changed: Charles Taylor was removed from power, exiled, indicted, and ultimately convicted of war crimes — a complete accountability arc. A peace deal was signed that held. Ellen Johnson Sirleaf was elected as Africa's first female head of state. Leymah Gbowee's model of cross-religious women's nonviolent peacebuilding has been replicated in conflict zones from Syria to South Sudan. Liberia has maintained fragile but real peace since 2003 — now more than 20 years. The Women of Liberia Mass Action for Peace changed the outcome of a civil war, making it one of the most documented and complete examples of nonviolent women's action producing historic political change.
